title: Learning to be uncertain before learning from data

data_scope.primary_benchmark: MNIST handwritten digits (4 vs. 9 binary subset)
data_scope.mnist_total_size: 70,000 images (full MNIST; exact subset count not stated in article)
data_scope.warm_up_data: synthetic random inputs and random labels, volume not specified
data_scope.article_type: Brief Communication — no novel empirical dataset; MNIST used for illustration only

core_conclusion: Neural networks are structurally overconfident at random initialization, before any real data are observed. A brief noise warm-up phase — training on random inputs and random labels — collapses initial overconfidence to chance level, yielding better-calibrated models with more reliable out-of-distribution detection. Miscalibration is reframed as an initial-condition problem, not solely a training-data or overfitting problem.
why_it_matters: Identifies random weight initialization as a previously overlooked root cause of miscalibration; offers a simple cost-free fix requiring no architectural changes or extra real data; directly improves safety-critical OOD detection; bridges machine learning with neuroscience (spontaneous pre-sensory neural activity).
why_nature: Conceptual novelty with broad cross-domain impact; cross-disciplinary resonance with neuroscience of biological development; safety-critical relevance to trustworthy AI; methodological elegance — minimal, theoretically motivated intervention explaining an empirical mystery with mechanistic clarity.
added_value_to_my_research: Noise warm-up before fine-tuning geospatial foundation models to reduce spatial overconfidence on unseen cities/regions
Calibrated initial uncertainty from noise warm-up as a reliable cold-start signal for spatial active learning data acquisition
Maximum-entropy initialization prior for robust OOD detection in anomalous spatiotemporal mobility and disease dynamics
Test noise warm-up ↔ training-data-composition interaction under geographic imbalance in multi-source GeoAI pipelines
Noise warm-up as initialization strategy for calibrated probabilistic uncertainty intervals in explainable spatial prediction models

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Initialization-dependent confidence miscalibration and its correction by noise warm-up. Four panels showing MNIST (4 vs. 9): (a) overconfident random-init network before training; (b) confidence collapse to chance level after noise warm-up; (c) spuriously high OOD confidence in standard network; (d) near-chance OOD confidence after noise warm-up.
